one_line_takeaway: EvoJail casts black-box jailbreak-prompt generation as an evolutionary multi-objective search (attack success + population diversity) driven by LLM-based instruction-fusion initialization and multi-granularity LLM crossover/mutation operators, achieving 93-99% ASR across five safety-tuned LLMs and topping 14 baselines in prompt diversity (0.488 vs. next-best 0.462) at comparable or lower query/token cost.
key_finding: On the five main target models, EvoJail achieves the highest ASR of any method: 99% (Llama-3.3-70B), 98% (Qwen2.5-14B), 98% (DeepSeek-R1-14B), 98% (GPT-3.5-Turbo), 93% (GPT-4o), improving over the best baseline by 6%, 4%, 1%, 6%, and 16% ASR respectively (JAIL-CON-CVT/CIT were the strongest baselines on most models but collapsed to 0-3% ASR on GPT-4o). Against the unmodified 'Base' prompts (0-25% ASR, 6-44% NRR), EvoJail lifts ASR to 93-99% and NRR to 90-100%. Across Llama-2-7B/13B/70B, EvoJail held 96-97% ASR and 97-99% NRR while all baselines degraded on larger models. On diversity (Table 4, GPT-4o as victim), EvoJail scored 0.488 average diversity versus a next-best of 0.462 (TAP) and 0.459 (PAIR); most heuristic baselines scored 0.004-0.392 -- this is the paper's basis for the abstract's '>5.6% improvement in diversity metrics' claim, achieved while maintaining 90% ASR / 88% NRR. Across 20 application fields (Table 5), EvoJail averaged 91.25% ASR and 98.65% NRR, with gaming highest at 96% ASR. Efficiency: EvoJail required ~75 queries and ~34,000 tokens per successful jailbreak versus ~140 queries/38,000 tokens (PAPILLON), ~200 queries/44,000 tokens (PAIR), ~270 queries/110,000 tokens (AutoDAN). Ablation on GPT-4o: full EvoJail 93% ASR/99% NRR; removing crossover drops ASR to 85% (-8); removing mutation drops ASR to 81% (-12); removing both drops ASR to 55% (-38) while NRR stays at 96%, indicating the fused initialization alone already achieves high non-rejection but crossover/mutation are needed for actual harmful-content elicitation. Cross-model transfer: prompts generated against GPT-4o (the hardest victim) transferred best to other models (avg ASR 62.75%, NRR 86.5%), while prompts generated against DeepSeek-R1-14B (weakest defender) transferred worst (avg ASR 27.25%).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is EvoJail, a black-box evolutionary jailbreak-prompt generation framework that formalizes jailbreak generation as a multi-objective optimization problem over a discrete, structured instruction space, maximizing a fitness function F(p) = w1*S(p) + w2*D(p) that jointly rewards attack success (safety-risk score S, from an inverted 1-5 Just-Eval score via an auxiliary GPT-4o-mini judge) and population novelty (diversity score D, 1 minus mean pairwise TF-IDF cosine similarity). EvoJail has three novel mechanisms distinct from prior evolutionary jailbreak work (AutoDAN, PAIR, JAIL-CON, etc., which the paper places in Related Work as prior art it builds on/compares against, not its own method): (1) instruction-fusion population initialization, where an auxiliary LLM (M_task) generates benign task instructions across 20 predefined application fields (200 total, 10 per field) and a second auxiliary LLM (M_fuse) implicitly embeds a harmful objective into each benign carrier task, analogized in the paper to malicious-code obfuscation (payload hidden in benign control flow); (2) an elite-hybrid evolutionary selection strategy that preserves the top alpha*N individuals (elitism, alpha=0.1) and fills the remainder via a 50/50 mix of roulette-wheel and tournament (k=3) selection; and (3) a family of four LLM-based mutation operators operating at word, sentence, semantic, and structural granularity, plus an LLM-based semantic crossover operator, all designed to avoid the linguistic-coherence breakage that token-level genetic operators cause in natural-language prompt spaces. The auxiliary LLM used for initialization, crossover, and mutation is DeepSeek-V3-0324; population size N=10, elitism rate alpha=0.1, crossover probability 0.5, mutation probability 0.1, iteration threshold t=10, w1=w2=0.5.
threat_model: Black-box adversary with API-only access to a target LLM M_tgt (no parameters, gradients, training data, or safety-policy internals); the adversary can issue natural-language prompts and observe responses, and may optionally use surrogate open-source LLMs for offline optimization. The adversary's goal is not a single jailbreak prompt but a diverse set P = {p1,...,pK} of prompts that (a) remain effective across successive versions of the same model family (adaptability) and (b) span distinct structural/semantic/field regions of prompt space rather than collapsing onto a few templates (diversity). This reflects realistic attacks on commercial closed models (e.g., GPT-4-class APIs) where only input-output behavior is observable.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations/observations: (1) EvoJail requires relatively longer runtime than heuristic (single-shot) jailbreak methods, though it is more efficient than other evolutionary methods (Conclusion). (2) The safety-risk scorer and the jailbreak-success judge both rely on an auxiliary LLM (GPT-4o-mini for scoring, GPT-4o for final success judgment), so results inherit that judge's own biases/errors -- the paper does not report inter-judge or human-agreement validation for these automated judgments. (3) Diversity is measured via TF-IDF cosine similarity in the fitness function but via sentence-embedding cosine similarity (Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B) in the reported evaluation -- these are two different diversity operationalizations, and the paper does not discuss whether optimizing the TF-IDF objective reliably transfers to the embedding-based diversity metric used for evaluation. (4) All experiments use publicly accessible models under research conditions; the authors explicitly withheld publishing generated jailbreaks and describe a responsible-disclosure process, which the paper frames as an ethical safeguard but which also means the released code/dataset scope is deliberately limited (dual-use risk acknowledged directly in Section 6). (5) Cross-model transferability is asymmetric and generally moderate (27-63% average ASR when transferring), meaning EvoJail's headline near-99% ASR figures are per-model-optimized, not a single universal prompt set.

datasets_used: JBB-Behaviors (Chao et al., 2024) -- 100 misuse behaviors across 10 categories
quantitative_results: ASR on Llama-3.3-70B: EvoJail 99% vs best baseline (JAIL-CON-CVT/CIT) 93%
ASR on Qwen2.5-14B: EvoJail 98% vs best baseline 94%
ASR on DeepSeek-R1-14B: EvoJail 98% vs best baseline 94%
ASR on GPT-3.5-Turbo: EvoJail 98% vs best baseline 92%
ASR on GPT-4o: EvoJail 93% vs best baseline (PAPILLON) 77%
Base (unmodified JBB-Behaviors) ASR range: 0-25%, NRR range: 6-44%
Diversity score (GPT-4o victim): EvoJail 0.488 vs PAIR 0.459, TAP 0.462, PAPILLON 0.392, most heuristic baselines 0.004-0.392
Field diversity (20 fields): average ASR 91.25%, average NRR 98.65%; gaming highest at 96% ASR
Query/token efficiency (GPT-4o): EvoJail ~75 queries/~34,000 tokens per success; PAPILLON ~140 queries/~38,000 tokens; PAIR ~200 queries/~44,000 tokens; AutoDAN ~270 queries/~110,000 tokens
Ablation (GPT-4o): full EvoJail 93% ASR/99% NRR; no-crossover 85% ASR (-8); no-mutation 81% ASR (-12); no-crossover-no-mutation 55% ASR (-38)/96% NRR
Cross-model transfer average ASR by source model: Llama-3.3-70B 36.75%, Qwen2.5-14B 40.75%, DeepSeek-R1-14B 27.25%, GPT-3.5-Turbo 35.75%, GPT-4o 62.75%
Llama-2 scale robustness: EvoJail held 96-97% ASR / 97-99% NRR across 7B/13B/70B while baselines degraded with scale
